Title: ITPK1 is an InsP6/ADP phosphotransferase that controls phosphate signaling in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MRP5-dependent InsP6 compartmentalization limits PP-InsP production; loss of MRP5 increases InsP7 and InsP8 in shoots and roots without obvious phosphate overaccumulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MRP5-mediated vacuolar InsP6 compartmentalization influences tissue levels of InsP7 and InsP8. 2. mrp5 mutants accumulate more PP-InsPs. 3. Despite higher PP-InsPs, Pi-dependent signaling output and shoot phosphate concentration are not strongly altered under tested conditions.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts upstream by controlling substrate accessibility rather than catalyzing PP-InsP synthesis.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be useful to modulate PP-InsP pool partitioning, but metabolic consequences appear context dependent and not sufficient alone to reprogram phosphate homeostasis.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulatory signal associated with phosphate signaling
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PHO2 loss elevates internal phosphate and strongly increases InsP8 and InsP7, demonstrating that PP-InsP accumulation tracks cellular Pi status; additive interaction with itpk1 suggests ITPK1 acts largely independently of PHO2.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: pho2-1 mutant profiling; CE-ESI-MS; PAGE; double mutant with itpk1
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pho2-1 shoots overaccumulate phosphate and show strongly elevated InsP8, with lesser increase of 5-InsP7 and 1/3-InsP7. 2. Lower InsPs are not similarly elevated. 3. itpk1 pho2 double mutants display additive phosphate overaccumulation, indicating largely independent functions.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because mutant analysis directly links cellular Pi status to PP-InsP levels, though PHO2 is not a PP-InsP metabolic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulation strongly affects phosphate accumulation but risks toxicity/homeostasis defects.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates InsP6 to generate 5-InsP7 under high ATP; reverses this reaction under low ATP/high ADP by transferring phosphate from 5-InsP7 to ADP to form ATP and InsP6.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Shoots > roots in Arabidopsis; also detected in roots
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulatory signal for phosphate homeostasis and phosphate starvation responses via SPX-PHR signaling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes Pi-dependent synthesis of 5-InsP7 and thereby InsP8; loss of ITPK1 reduces 5-InsP7 and compromises InsP8 accumulation after Pi resupply, causing phosphate overaccumulation and misregulated Pi starvation signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (itpk1); complementation with genomic ITPK1; CE-ESI-MS metabolite profiling; PAGE; recombinant enzyme assay; NMR kinetics; grafting; genetic interaction analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in various tissues including shoots and roots
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Activity/function is especially determinant in shoots for regulation of shoot phosphate accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphate sufficiency, phosphate starvation, phosphate resupply; ATP/ADP ratio
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ITPK1 is required in planta for Pi-dependent synthesis of 5-InsP7 and contributes to 1/3-InsP7 and InsP8 accumulation. 2. Recombinant ITPK1 converts InsP6 to 5-InsP7 at high ATP and specifically dephosphorylates 5-InsP7 to InsP6 while producing ATP at high ADP/low ATP. 3. Loss of ITPK1 causes shoot phosphate overaccumulation, misregulation of Pi-starvation responses, and defective PP-InsP dynamics, with strong functional importance in shoots.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ITPK1 does not phosphorylate 1-InsP7 or 5-InsP7 to InsP8 in vitro. It is also required for synthesis of a root-specific presumptive novel PP-InsP4 isomer and affects multiple lower InsP intermediates. Catalytic dead ITPK1 mutants lose both kinase and ADP phosphotransferase activities.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to tune phosphate accumulation and phosphate-use signaling, but perturbation causes strong P overaccumulation and growth/root defects; engineering should preserve balanced ITPK1 activity rather than simple knockout.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive phosphate overaccumulation, smaller plant size, shorter roots, altered Pi-starvation gene expression
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics-like targeted CE-ESI-MS/MS; [3H]inositol HPLC profiling; no public accession reported

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: 5-InsP7; possibly InsP6 for 1/3-InsP7 formation in planta support discussed
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates 5-InsP7 to produce InsP8; in planta data also support contribution to recovery of 1/3-InsP7 after Pi resupply.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Shoots and roots, higher in shoots
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulatory signal for phosphate homeostasis and repression of phosphate starvation responses
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes Pi-resupply-induced InsP8 accumulation; loss of VIH2 lowers InsP8 and causes 5-InsP7 accumulation, supporting 5-InsP7 as major substrate for InsP8 synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: vih2-4 mutant analysis; CE-ESI-MS metabolite profiling; PAGE; genetic interaction with itpk1
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphate sufficiency, phosphate starvation, phosphate resupply; ATP and Pi levels influence VIH activity as discussed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VIH2 is required for robust InsP8 synthesis in response to Pi resupply. 2. vih2 mutants accumulate 5-InsP7, indicating that 5-InsP7 is the major substrate for InsP8 production in shoots. 3. Genetic combination with itpk1 aggravates phosphate-homeostasis defects, showing pathway interdependence.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes VIH2 over VIH1 in core Arabidopsis mutant analyses, although both VIHs contribute to InsP8 synthesis. Lower InsP forms are little affected in vih2 mutants.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential lever for altering phosphate signaling via InsP8, but disruption causes phosphate-homeostasis imbalance and growth defects when combined with other pathway lesions.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbed phosphate signaling/homeostasis; enhanced defects in itpk1 vih2 double mutant
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted CE-ESI-MS/MS; PAGE; no public accession reported

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions as an InsP6 kinase and partially compensates for loss of ITPK1 in maintaining phosphate homeostasis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Single itpk2-2 has little effect on PP-InsPs or phosphate phenotype, but double loss with ITPK1 causes severe growth retardation and strongly elevated shoot phosphate, indicating partial redundancy.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double-mutant analysis with itpk1; CE-ESI-MS profiling of itpk2-2; phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pi-containing substrate; phosphate regimes in hydroponics
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ITPK2 alone plays a minor role under normal conditions. 2. ITPK2 can partially compensate for loss of ITPK1. 3. itpk1 itpk2 double mutants show severe growth retardation and strong shoot P overaccumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as core because double-mutant experiments directly test its metabolic contribution to phosphate homeostasis downstream of PP-InsP synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suggests redundancy should be considered in engineering phosphate signaling; combined perturbation is deleterious.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe growth retardation and excessive shoot phosphate when combined with itpk1
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted CE-ESI-MS/MS; no public accession reported

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PHR transcription factor family; MYB-CC domain
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Signal perceived through SPX proteins to repress Pi starvation responses
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: PHR1 is a core downstream transcriptional effector through which ITPK1-dependent PP-InsP synthesis controls phosphate signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Pi starvation-induced genes including SPX1, IPS1, PHT1;1, PHT1;2, PHT1;8, PHT1;9
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates Pi starvation-induced genes when released from SPX repression
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation; repressed through SPX-PP-InsP association
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Phosphate status; SPX proteins bound by PP-InsPs
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for full manifestation of ITPK1-dependent phosphate overaccumulation and Pi-starvation gene misregulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic interaction analysis with itpk1 and phl1; qRT-PCR of PSI genes; shoot phosphate measurements
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not markedly induced by Pi starvation according to supplemental table
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Loss of PHR1 attenuates the phosphate overaccumulation phenotype of itpk1. 2. ITPK1-dependent control of Pi-starvation gene expression acts largely through PHR1/PHL1. 3. PHR1 transcript itself is not strongly Pi regulated under tested conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms repressive complex with SPX proteins in presence of PP-InsPs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts downstream of PP-InsP signaling rather than catalyzing metabolite synthesis directly.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulation may tune phosphate responses, but broad transcriptional effects make precise modulation preferable to full loss-of-function.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PHR-like transcription factor family; MYB-CC domain
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Signal associated with repression of Pi starvation responses through SPX-PHR module
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts with PHR1 as a major downstream transcriptional effector of ITPK1-dependent PP-InsP signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Pi starvation-induced genes overlapping with PHR1-controlled PSI genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates Pi-starvation response genes when not repressed by SPX-PP-InsP signaling
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation within phosphate signaling network
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Phosphate status; SPX proteins/PP-InsPs via PHR module
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes with PHR1 to the ITPK1-dependent phosphate overaccumulation phenotype and Pi-starvation gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Triple mutant analysis phr1 phl1 itpk1; qRT-PCR; shoot phosphate measurements
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PHL1 cooperates with PHR1 in mediating ITPK1-dependent phosphate signaling. 2. Loss of PHR1/PHL1 attenuates the itpk1 phosphate phenotype. 3. Some genes such as PHT1;8 remain misregulated, indicating additional control layers.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions in PHR transcriptional module repressed by SPX proteins
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used genetically to define dependency of ITPK1 signaling on central phosphate-starvation TFs.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for phosphate-signaling engineering, but broad regulatory pleiotropy is expected.
